reasoning: The baseline probability of the S&P 500 dropping more than 5% in a single day is historically very low (typically ~1% for a random 1-month period unless there is an ongoing severe financial crisis). The prediction market priced this event at 8% on November 13, 2025. Since there are no indications of a >5% index drop having occurred between November 13 and today (November 23), the event still has not occurred, and the time remaining for it to happen has decreased by about a third (from ~33 trading days to ~25). Prediction markets often overprice extreme tail risks, typically maintaining a floor of 3-8% for catastrophic options. Factoring in the time decay over the past 10 days and a slight correction for prediction market tail-risk bias, the true probability is estimated to be around 5%. There is no evidence in recent November news snippets of an ongoing crash that would significantly elevate this risk compared to the historical average.
